title: Public Works Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Public Works Committee met on May 11, 2026, approving minutes and reports, awarding a $126,900 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel, adopting a resolution supporting state transportation funding, and directing staff to establish $100/day fines for illegal sanitary sewer discharges. The committee also approved construction agreements for Military Road utilities and construction services for upcoming road projects.

agenda.item: Acknowledge April 2026 Water and Sewer Permits

discussions.body: Michael reported the village received a Local Road Improvement Grant for Birch Street reconstruction between Schofield Avenue and Community Center Drive, originally a 2029 project that will likely move to 2028. The grant covers over 50% of the estimated $1.5 million project cost. He noted the village has received over $9 million in grant funding over the past 4-5 years. Discussion included Weston Avenue road repairs by Integrity Grading and Excavating for settling over sewer trenches, and upcoming repairs on Schofield Avenue and Fuller Street for manhole issues and concrete pitting.

Staff reported road limits were lifted April 29th. Crews performed dewatering operations near Weston Avenue tributary for approximately one week due to flooding. A culvert failure on Jones Street required installation of a third 18-inch culvert. Equipment maintenance included removing plow equipment from trucks and servicing mowers. Two Mac tandem axle chassis have been ordered with upfitter items. Salt supply discussed - 1,500 tons ordered for November delivery with current stock adequate.

Josh reported significant clear water infiltration issues, with April showing 37 million gallons pumped into the system but 53-54 million gallons treated at the wastewater plant - approximately 15 million gallons of clear water. Kathleen lift station showed clear water from sump pump discharge. In-pipe Technologies will conduct INI study when dry weather permits. Well 2 rehab started that day with Carry, and tower chemical cleaning planned.

The water utility reported net operating income of $482,911 for 2025, consistent with 2024's $490,000. Water loss was 6%. Utility value increased to nearly $34 million with $6.7 million in infrastructure placed in service. PAS settlement funds of $1.44 million received. Utility serves approximately 6,100 customers - 5,250 in Weston, 830 in Rothschild. Motion to acknowledge passed unanimously.

Michael presented the League of Wisconsin Municipalities sample resolution supporting long-term transportation funding solutions. He noted the village has decreased public works budgets recently and Wisconsin remains affordable compared to neighboring states for vehicle ownership. Motion to adopt Resolution 2026-014 passed unanimously.

King Gravel submitted the low bid at $4.23/ton for crushing approximately 30,000 tons of recycled material at Ryan Street site, totaling $126,900. PJ and Michael's Materials also bid at $4.25/ton. Michael noted this saves approximately $11/cubic yard compared to City of Wausau costs, representing about $130,000 in savings on 2026 projects. Motion to award to King Gravel passed unanimously.

Committee discussed enforcement of illegal sump pump discharges to sanitary sewers contributing to 15 million gallons of clear water treatment costs. Josh noted one Kathleen station resident admitted to illegal discharge. Committee debated fine amounts between $100-$1,000/day. Consensus reached on $100/day for first offenses, with escalation for willful violations. Motion directing staff to create enforcement language with fines not exceeding $100/day passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Water and Sewer Connection Agreement - Radkey Avenue
discussions.body: Michael presented agreement allowing temporary water/sewer connection for a duplex at 2230 Radkey Avenue via long lateral until Radkey Avenue reconstruction. Committee added language requiring reconstruction no later than 2029. Motion to recommend approval with 2029 deadline passed unanimously.

Agreement with Village of Rothschild for utility work on Military Road (Wolfman to Helin Street). Weston's share is $94,860 for sanitary sewer ($6,156) and water main ($88,704) relocation due to storm sewer conflicts. Steam Construction is the contractor. Motion to recommend approval passed unanimously.

For Alderson/Jelinek intersection roundabout, MSA proposed $37,315 versus Clark Dietz at $48,910 - MSA recommended due to lower cost and roundabout design involvement. For Bladel Avenue, MSA proposed $70,400 versus Clark Dietz at $73,730 - Clark Dietz recommended due to full-time experienced inspector versus MSA's summer intern. Motion to approve both recommendations passed unanimously.

actionItems: Award 2026 recycled asphalt crushing contract to King Gravel at $4.23/ton ($126,900 estimated)
Adopt Resolution 2026-014 supporting state transportation funding
Direct staff to create enforcement language for sanitary sewer discharge violations with $100/day fines
Recommend village board approve Radkey Avenue temporary utility connection agreement with 2029 reconstruction deadline
Recommend village board approve Military Road construction agreement with Rothschild ($94,860 Weston share)
Award Alderson/Jelinek construction services to MSA ($37,315) and Bladel Avenue to Clark Dietz ($73,730)
Schedule Bladel Avenue special assessment meeting for May 28, 2026 at 6:00 PM
Begin water main flushing next week with notices mailed to residents
Norcon to begin Schofield Avenue concrete repairs starting Wednesday/Thursday

title: Tourism Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Tourism Commission held its reorganization meeting, electing new officers and approving a $5,000 grant for the Taste and Glow Balloonfest. The commission learned that a competing hot air balloon event in Marshfield was canceled, potentially increasing attendance at the Weston event.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
New business - Grant request for Taste and Glow Balloonfest

discussions.body: Trustee Erling was nominated for Chair and approved unanimously. Cushing was nominated for Vice Chair, seconded by Jackson, and approved unanimously. Renee was nominated and approved unanimously as Secretary.
Minutes from the April 21st meeting were approved unanimously. The motion was seconded by Renee.

discussions.item: Grant Request - Taste and Glow Balloonfest
discussions.body: Brandon Jill of 3308 Green Pastures Lane represented the Taste and Glow Balloonfest, reporting estimated attendance of 11,000-12,000 last year. He announced that a competing Marshfield balloon event was canceled due to permit issues, making theirs the sole hot air balloon event in the area. A commissioner questioned room usage, noting the application showed 20 fewer rooms than last year. The commission requested better tracking of hotel room data for future applications. A motion to approve $5,000 (matching last year's grant) was made and passed unanimously, with Jackson seconding.

Renee reported attending the Alice in Dairyland dinner at Red Rock Orchard in Edgar, describing the venue and meeting the candidates. She noted the winner, Anastasia, sat at their table. This was the first time Marathon County hosted the event since the 1970s. The candidates visited local attractions including Mullen's Cheese and were impressed with the community.

actionItems: Approved $5,000 grant for Taste and Glow Balloonfest
Taste and Glow organizers to provide hotel room usage data in their post-event report
Next meeting scheduled for July 20th at 4:00 PM (tentative)

title: Plan Commission
overview: The Village of Weston Plan Commission approved a site plan for the new Mountain Bay Metropolitan Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive, and adopted a new salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection districts. Both items passed unanimously.

agenda.item: Call to order and roll call
Site plan review for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility at 3600 Community Center Drive
Creation of salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
April 2026 staff approved certified survey map and site plan
Planning and development department monthly project update

discussions.item: Site plan review for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility
discussions.body: Staff presented the site plan for the new police department and municipal court at 3600 Community Center Drive. Chief Jeremy explained the building was rotated to address challenges with a gas pipeline running through the property, which reduced fill costs significantly. The building is designed for 50-60 officer capacity for future growth. Discussion included questions about an alternate storage shed placement to allow for future expansion, landscaping compliance, and gas line construction requirements. A motion to recommend approval to the board of trustees with staff conditions 2-5 passed unanimously.

discussions.item: Salt storage shed inspection program for wellhead protection district
discussions.body: Jennifer presented a proposed inspection program requiring two annual inspections per facility - a warm weather inspection focused on structural integrity and a winter operational inspection focused on material handling practices. The program includes a standardized inspection worksheet similar to DOT requirements. Staff noted one existing facility near the municipal well on Sternberg has never gone through the wellhead protection conditional use process and should be contacted. The commission approved the program with an amendment to require reporting back to both the Plan Commission and Public Works Committee. Motion passed unanimously.
Motion to approve the April 13, 2026 meeting minutes as presented passed unanimously with no discussion.

Jennifer announced the first meeting of the joint Plan Commission/Village Board steering committee for the comprehensive plan is scheduled for Monday, June 1st at 5:00 p.m. She will send email confirmation and calendar invites to commissioners.

publicComment: Jim Pensson of 5002 Arrow Street, Weston, spoke via online connection. He urged the commission to exercise common sense on landscaping requirements for the police department project to avoid excessive taxpayer costs similar to the Green Heck Turner building. He also requested that the salt shed inspection program include annual reporting on inspection results for the limited number of facilities in wellhead protection zones.
actionItems: Site plan for Mountain Bay Metro Police Department and Municipal Court facility recommended for approval to Board of Trustees with staff conditions
Salt storage shed inspection program approved for implementation with reporting to both Plan Commission and Public Works Committee
Staff to reach out to existing salt storage facility on Sternberg that has not gone through wellhead protection conditional use process
Staff to report inspection findings to Plan Commission and Public Works around May 2027
First comprehensive plan steering committee meeting scheduled for June 1st at 5:00 p.m.
Future agenda item: electric vehicle charging stations expected in fall

title: Health & Human Services Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Health & Human Services Committee held an organizational meeting for new board members, featuring educational presentations from the Health Department, Social Services, Veteran Services, and Aging & Disability Resource Center. No policy votes were taken; the meeting focused on introducing department structures, missions, and budgets to orient new committee members.

agenda.item: Call to order and remote participation confirmation
Approval of April 1st committee meeting minutes
Operational functions required by statute, ordinance, resolution or policy
Review of standing committee ordinance 2.04 sub5 - missions, duties, and responsibilities
Next meeting date discussion and adjournment

discussions.body: Supervisor Cavalli made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Vice Chair Rosenberg. The motion carried with all in favor by voice vote.

discussions.item: Review of standing committee ordinance and structure
discussions.body: Administrator Lance Leonard explained the committee's jurisdiction under ordinance 2.04 sub5, covering the Health Department, Social Services, Veteran Services, North Central Health Care, and ADRC. He outlined reporting relationships with statutory boards including the Social Services Board, Veteran Service Commission, Transportation Coordinating Committee, and Board of Health. Chair Boots noted the committee will focus on developing a new strategic plan by December.

Health Officer Caitlyn Suginaka presented the department's mission of preventing disease, promoting health, and protecting from environmental hazards. The department has 37.6 FTE managing over 1,200 licenses, communicable disease response, family health programs including Nurse Family Partnership, and a new homeless system response position. She highlighted public health vending machines offering free resources including tick kits. The 2026 budget shows approximately $5 million total with about half from tax levy.

Administrator Leonard presented on behalf of Director Christa, describing the department's divisions: child welfare (CPS and youth justice), children's long-term support, economic support, and child support. With nearly 140 FTE, it's the second largest department. He highlighted the successful Family Keys housing stability pilot program addressing housing instability among child welfare and youth justice populations. The 2026 budget shows over $22 million total with about $7 million from tax levy.

Veteran Service Officer Aaron Galindo reported the department increased from 2.75 to 3.0 FTE. Customer interactions jumped from 5,900 to 8,149 in 2025. The department helps veterans access VA healthcare and disability benefits, with $44 million annually flowing to local veterans and $2.6 million in retroactive payments in 2025. Key initiatives include surviving spouse benefits (DIC), housing stability with Center for Veterans Issues, and suicide prevention - noting 7 of 30 county suicides in 2025 were veterans. Supervisor Kremes asked about staffing adequacy; Galindo acknowledged the office is busy but managing well with appointment wait times reduced to under 3 weeks.

Executive Director Mike Ray presented on the four-county ADRC serving Marathon, Lincoln, Langlade, and Wood counties. Services include information and assistance, caregiver support, dementia care, falls prevention, benefit specialists, and Meals on Wheels. He described significant financial challenges with nutrition costs up 40-45% over four years while federal funding remained flat. The Older Americans Act hasn't been reauthorized in over two years. Marathon County's 2026 contribution is $413,135. The organization relies on approximately 385-400 volunteers.
Vice Chair Rosenberg caught an error in the stated next meeting date. The meeting was corrected from May 29th to June 3rd at 3 PM.

actionItems: April 1st meeting minutes approved
North Central Healthcare to be scheduled for separate full presentation before budget process
ADRC Executive Director Mike Ray to present annual report with 2025 data to full board
Committee members to email Chair Boots and Administrator Leonard with future agenda item requests
Chair Boots to present strategic plan information at upcoming meetings
Next meeting scheduled for June 3rd at 3 PM

title: Board of Trustees
overview: The Village of Weston Board of Trustees approved increased park and recreation impact fees for new residential development, passed ordinances regulating mobile food vendors and e-bikes, and discussed ongoing challenges with the farmers market's new location at Kennedy Park. The board also adjourned the Board of Review to June 15, 2026 and approved various license renewals for the upcoming year.

agenda.item: Board of Review called to order and adjourned to June 15, 2026
Board of Trustees meeting called to order with roll call
Public hearing on park and recreation impact fees (Ordinance 26-010 and Resolution 2026-015)
Approval of minutes from previous meetings
Acknowledge reports from boards, committees, and commissions with chair updates
Consent agenda - vouchers and license renewals
Ordinances including mobile food vendor, nuisances, and e-bikes
Discussion on farmers market location at Kennedy Park

discussions.item: Public Hearing - Park and Recreation Impact Fees
discussions.body: The board held a public hearing on increasing park and recreation impact fees from $300/$250 per unit to $761 for single family, $563 for duplex, and $495 for multifamily units, consistent with the 2022 public facilities needs assessment. Jim Pitel spoke during public comment questioning the lack of detailed cost data to justify the fees. Trustee Barb supported the increase, noting population growth requires more park space. Ordinance 26-010 was approved with motion by Lopsorrell, second by Zagami, passing unanimously. Resolution 2026-015 amending the fee schedule was approved with motion by Hermling, second by Clark, passing unanimously.

Chief Finkey explained this ordinance creates a regional reciprocal inspection agreement between Safer and municipalities including Marshfield, Wisconsin Rapids, Stevens Point, and Clover Belt. Under the new system, a food truck inspected by any participating department receives a sticker recognized by all, eliminating the need for multiple inspections across communities. Motion by Lopsorrell, second by Zagami, passed unanimously.

The board debated amendments to the remote meeting attendance policy. After significant discussion, the board voted to remove language requiring specific health, safety, or welfare justifications for remote attendance and remove language prohibiting remote attendance for vacation or travel. The policy was amended to simply require 12-hour advance notice to the administrator and clerk, being on camera, and prohibition from closed sessions. Amendment motion by Beck, second by Clark passed with Hermling and Motti opposed. The amended policy then passed with the same two opposed.

The board held discussion-only on the farmers market's new location at Kennedy Park after losing their previous Camp Phillips/Scoffield Avenue location to development. Concerns were raised about parking on grass areas along Alderson creating safety issues and potential damage to fields. Renee explained the market has grown from 25 to 53 vendor stalls and generates positive revenue through vendor fees, grants, and token programs. Mount Miller Park was discussed but rejected due to poor visibility. No action was taken; the board agreed to continue monitoring the situation.

discussions.item: Secondhand Article Dealer License - Scoffield Coin and Hobby
discussions.body: The board approved a secondhand article dealer and secondhand jewelry dealer license for Scoffield Coin and Hobby LLC at 5703 Jamar Street. Chief Hunt explained the business deals with precious metals and large dollar transactions requiring tracking for stolen merchandise protection. The motion was amended to require satisfactory completion of background check and receipt of occupancy permit since the building is still under construction. Passed unanimously with amendments.

The president appointed Stephanie Daniels (currently on Finance and HR) and Golden Mua to the CLIPS committee. Motion by Hermling, second by Lopsorrell, passed unanimously. The board noted openings remain on Parks, Finance/HR (two), and CLIPS (one). The board agreed to include applicant resumes in future board packets.

publicComment: Jim Pitel of 52nd Street spoke during public comment criticizing the village for spending $108,000 on outside engineering consultants rather than filling the vacant engineering technician position. He questioned why Lisa Beck was introduced as a Safer board member at an April Fire Department meeting before being officially appointed by the board, calling it 'inappropriate government business taking action outside of this boardroom.' He also criticized the lack of detailed information on Kennedy Park funding in the board packet and called for the administrator to be held more accountable.
actionItems: Board of Review adjourned to June 15, 2026
Park and recreation impact fees increased to $761 single family, $563 duplex, $495 multifamily
Mobile food vendor reciprocal inspection ordinance approved
E-bike/e-moto ordinance amendments approved
Nuisance ordinance amendments approved
Remote meeting attendance policy amended to remove justification requirements
Secondhand dealer license approved for Scoffield Coin and Hobby pending background check and occupancy permit
Stephanie Daniels and Golden Mua appointed to CLIPS committee
All annual license renewals approved for 2026-2027 term
Intergovernmental service agreement to be developed for building inspection assistance to Town of Ringle tornado damage
Speed limit study on Weston Avenue to be brought back to board
First comprehensive plan steering committee meeting scheduled for June 1 at 6:00 PM
Applicant resumes for committee appointments to be included in future board packets

title: Regular Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Board held an adjourned organizational meeting where committee appointments were approved, two at-large members were elected to the Executive Committee, and a resolution was unanimously passed to reduce landfill tipping fees for debris from the recent tornado and flooding disaster affecting Ringle and other communities. The board also approved consent agenda items and resolutions related to out-of-home placement reserves and social services budget amendments.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance and silent moment of reflection
Reading of notice and roll call
Committee appointments by County Board Chair
Election of at-large Executive Committee members
Consent agenda items C10 through C14A
Resolution 26-26: Landfill tipping fee modification for disaster debris
Motion to suspend rules for disaster tipping fee resolution

discussions.body: Chair Gibbs presented committee appointments developed with Vice Chair Boots, emphasizing goals of increasing diversity of perspectives, developing leadership among newer supervisors (34% of the board is in first or second term), and accommodating supervisor preferences. The motion was made by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Crims. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

discussions.item: Election of at-large Executive Committee members
discussions.body: Three supervisors were nominated: Supervisor Hapa (nominated by Supervisor Mask), Supervisor Leur (nominated by Supervisor Rosenberg), and Supervisor Arstead (nominated by Vice Chair Boots). Supervisor Rosenberg explained her nomination of Supervisor Leur, citing her advocacy for the county's youngest and oldest residents. Paper ballots were distributed with supervisors selecting two names. Supervisor Hapa and Supervisor Arstead were elected to the Executive Committee.

Administrator Leonard and Solid Waste Director Hagen Busher presented a proposal from the Solid Waste Management Board to reduce tipping fees for disaster debris from the recent tornado and flooding. The state waived its $13.10 fee, Ringle is waiving its $2.33 fee, and the county proposed reducing fees to $36.94 per ton (the commercial rate minus state and local fees) to cover operational costs. Public speakers Jessica Toasty and Matt Haybeck requested the county waive fees entirely, noting 44 homes affected and insurance adjusters still pending. After extensive discussion about costs, timing, and policy implications, the board unanimously approved suspending rules to hear the resolution, then unanimously passed Resolution 26-26.
Items C10 through C14A were approved on consent. Motion by Supervisor Cavelli, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously.
Motion by Supervisor Morash, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.
Motion by Supervisor P, seconded by Supervisor Robinson. The motion carried but was not unanimous.

publicComment: Two members of the public were granted special dispensation to speak on the landfill tipping fee resolution. Jessica Toasty asked Marathon County to waive fees entirely for disaster debris, noting the state, DNR, and Ringle are all waiving their fees, and emphasized that affected residents are facing insurance deductibles and other costs not covered by insurance. She requested a six-month waiver period. Matt Haybeck spoke on behalf of residents whose homes need demolition, noting some of the approximately 44 affected homes are still waiting for insurance adjusters, and asked for relief for underinsured residents.
actionItems: Committee appointments approved as presented by Chair Gibbs and Vice Chair Boots
Supervisor Hapa and Supervisor Arstead elected to at-large Executive Committee seats
Resolution 26-26 passed unanimously reducing landfill tipping fees for disaster debris through June 17, 2025
Staff directed to work with Community Foundation on potential reimbursement mechanism for unreimbursed insurance costs
Solid Waste Management Board to develop long-term disaster debris tipping fee policy within six months
Resolution 24-26 approved for out-of-home placement reserve increase
Resolution 25-26 approved for social services budget amendment

title: Environmental Resources Committee Meeting
overview: The Marathon County Environmental Resources Committee approved two rezoning petitions and voted to recommend a limited fee waiver for tornado-affected properties to the county board. The committee also received educational presentations from the Solid Waste, Parks Recreation and Forestry, and Conservation Planning and Zoning departments.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Public hearing: Tim Veland rezoning petition (Urban Residential/General Agriculture to Rural Residential, Town of Green Valley)
Public hearing: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture, Town of McMillan)
Consideration of fee waivers for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather
Educational presentation: Solid Waste Department overview
Educational presentation: Parks, Recreation, and Forestry Department overview
Educational presentation: Conservation, Planning, and Zoning Department overview

discussions.body: Supervisor Sefeld made a motion to approve the minutes, seconded by Supervisor Drebeck. The motion carried unanimously with no discussion.

discussions.item: Tim Veland rezoning petition (Town of Green Valley)
discussions.body: Land Use Specialist Garrett Pogle presented a petition to rezone land from Urban Residential and General Agriculture to Rural Residential for a proposed land division. The Town of Green Valley recommended approval on March 10, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Drebeck moved to approve based on findings of fact and the town's approval, seconded by Supervisor Kurt. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz rezoning petition (Town of McMillan)
discussions.body: Garrett Pogle presented a petition to rezone from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture. Supervisor Leur asked about the conservancy designation and potential loss of natural areas. Pogle explained the property contains a home and horse riding stable, with no natural areas being preserved - the rezone would make existing structures conforming uses. The Town of McMillan recommended approval on April 20, 2026. No public testimony was offered. Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve, seconded by Supervisor Drew. Motion carried unanimously.

discussions.item: Fee waivers for April 17, 2026 severe weather impacts
discussions.body: Director Lori Mskimmons presented a proposal to waive county zoning and septic fees for properties impacted by the April 17 tornado affecting towns of Ringle, Easton, and villages of Kronenwetter and Weston. Up to 149 parcels were potentially affected. Supervisor Drew questioned where to draw the line for future events. Supervisor Drebeck raised concerns about fairness to property owners not in declared disaster areas. Eric Vogel asked about homeowners insurance coverage. After debate, Supervisor Sefeld moved to approve the full resolution. Supervisor Drebeck then offered an amendment to limit fee waivers to only reconnect permits ($160 each) for existing septic systems, excluding other zoning or new system fees. The amendment passed and the amended resolution was approved to forward to the county board, though not unanimously.

Director Dave Hagen Busher provided an overview of the landfill operations, explaining the facility has operated since 1980 and accepts about 1,000 tons daily. He highlighted the 2023 partnership with Vidi Energy to convert landfill gas to renewable natural gas, significant capital expenses for expansions and closures ($4-6 million per project), and upcoming state-mandated leachate treatment requirements by 2032. Marilyn Bend praised the department for keeping private landfill costs in line.

discussions.item: Parks, Recreation, and Forestry Department presentation
discussions.body: County Forest Administrator Tom Lovelin presented on behalf of Director Jamie, covering the department's 44 full-time and 120 seasonal staff serving both county and city facilities. He noted Nine Mile County Forest generates $3.25 million for the local economy from out-of-town visitors. Supervisor Sefeld announced Lovelin's retirement effective June 1st after nearly 30 years of service. The committee thanked him for his service.

actionItems: Rezoning from Urban Residential/General Agriculture to Rural Residential for James Offer and David Tract in Town of Green Valley approved and forwarded to County Board
Rezoning from Conservancy Recreation to General Agriculture for Jeffrey and Cynthia Schmidtz in Town of McMillan approved and forwarded to County Board
Limited fee waiver for septic reconnect permits only ($160) for properties impacted by April 17, 2026 severe weather forwarded to County Board for consideration
Staff to track costs and requests related to severe weather fee waivers for future policy development

title: Parks Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Parks Committee deferred discussion on allowing dogs at the aquatic center due to the chair's absence, approved installation of new kayak launch signs along the Plover River, and received updates on Kennedy Park fundraising and trail maintenance. Public commenters expressed opposition to dogs at the pool and criticized the 4 p.m. meeting time and Kennedy Park financial transparency.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Yellow Banks disc golf course update
Dog Day at the aquatic center
Graffiti on the Oaklair River Trail
Topics for future meetings and staff remarks

discussions.body: Minutes from the April 27, 2026 meeting were approved with a motion and second. The motion passed unanimously with no discussion.

Director Sean Oster provided a timeline of the Kennedy Park project from the 2023 comprehensive plan approval through the May 9th ribbon cutting. Committee member Scott asked about village capital dollars, and Oster clarified no village funds are currently earmarked as they were pulled pending the master plan. The Friends of Kennedy Park currently have approximately $84-85,000 at the community foundation toward a $117,000 goal for field lights. Oster noted a public check presentation is planned at the June tournament. A committee member emphasized that Friends of Kennedy Park is a volunteer organization that deserves grace and that the 10-year timeline for the master plan allows for changes.

discussions.item: Yellow Banks disc golf course update
discussions.body: Dan presented photos of fallen trees that were removed from the river and showed images of wood mat installation over gas pipelines to protect them during equipment access. The mats were provided by TC Energy (formerly ANR pipeline/ATC) and some extra mats may be used on other trail projects.

discussions.item: Dog Day at the aquatic center
discussions.body: Discussion was deferred to the next meeting because Committee Chair Katrina was absent due to an unforeseen issue. A public commenter named Andy from 6504 [street not fully captured] expressed opposition, stating animals should not be allowed in public pools and belong in rivers and lakes instead.

discussions.item: Graffiti on the Oaklair River Trail
discussions.body: Staff explained challenges removing graffiti from concrete and benches along the trail due to limited equipment access. The pressure washer on a tandem axle trailer cannot fit on the narrow bridges. Committee discussed anti-vandalism coatings and sealer options. Lisa suggested volunteer work days, noting community members love the trail. Staff will investigate cleaning options and potentially address benches one at a time during off-season.

Dan presented version seven of proposed signage for kayak launches along the Plover River. Signs will be installed at Highway J, Ross Avenue, and Yellow Banks in Weston, plus locations in Schofield. Funding comes from Marathon County Parks Foundation and state grants at no cost to the village. Dan noted the Highway J launch may be closed all season due to bridge replacement. Motion to approve sign installation passed unanimously.

Staff asked about interest in food allergy awareness signage at playgrounds after receiving an email request. Signs would be provided free and encourage keeping food at picnic tables rather than on play equipment. Committee agreed to bring this back for further discussion. Staff also announced the Mock Miller Park master plan process is beginning with Rettler, with a kickoff meeting scheduled for next Tuesday.

publicComment: Two public comments were offered. Andy from 6504 [street] spoke in person opposing dogs at the aquatic center, stating animals don't belong in public pools. Jim Pensan (appearing as 'Vern M' online) from 5002 Street Weston criticized the 4 p.m. meeting time as discouraging public participation and questioned Kennedy Park financial reporting, calling the $8,700 figure 'too coincidental' and asking about a $10,000 grant and glow event funds. He suggested the Friends of Kennedy Park may be 'frauds' for not sharing detailed finances.
actionItems: Minutes from April 27, 2026 approved
Dog Day at aquatic center discussion deferred to June meeting
Staff to investigate graffiti removal options and anti-vandalism coatings for Oaklair River Trail
Kayak launch signs approved for installation at village locations
Food allergy awareness signage to be brought back for future discussion
Mock Miller Park master plan kickoff meeting with Rettler scheduled for next Tuesday
Bike and pedestrian plan open house scheduled for June 11, 5-7 p.m.
Next meeting scheduled for June 22, 2026

title: Finance and Human Resources Committee
overview: The Village of Weston Finance and Human Resources Committee held an informational meeting focused on a detailed budget presentation from the Municipal Clerk's office covering elections, licensing, tax collection, and pet licensing operations. The committee acknowledged routine financial reports and approved previous meeting minutes, with no controversial items or significant policy decisions.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous Finance and HR meeting
TIF 1 and TIF 2 detail reports for April
Educational presentation - Municipal Clerk's budget overview
Staff remarks and farmers market discussion

discussions.item: Approval of minutes from previous meeting
discussions.body: Motion to approve made by Ernling, seconded by Daniels. Passed unanimously with no discussion.

Jessica from finance discussed the timeline for TIF closure, anticipating October/November completion. She explained the CDA will need to pass a resolution to call the bonds, which are due October 1st. The village will notify US Bank as associate trust and DTC will handle payments to bondholders. A trustee asked about final numbers, and Jessica confirmed they will be able to pay off the debt.
Motion to acknowledge the April budget status report, TIF reports, and legal details report made by Daniels, seconded by Soaper. Passed unanimously.

Clerk Pam presented a comprehensive overview of the clerk's office operations with staff members Sarah (full-time) and Leah (part-time). She covered election administration including Badger Book electronic poll books ($11,000 for four units), explaining postage costs increase from $6,000 to $15,000 in presidential election years due to absentee ballot volume. She noted 84% of tax collection ($22 million) comes through walk-ins at the clerk's office. Pet licensing was brought back in-house from Pet Data in 2025, collecting $17,480 through March with $8,067 retained by the village. The clerk noted newsletter printing and mailing costs total approximately $30,000 annually for four issues to 5,089 postal patrons.

A trustee asked about the pet licensing fee increase from $10 to $20 for neutered dogs. Pam explained Marathon County raised fees because they pay approximately $100,000 annually to the Humane Society but only collected $65,000 from municipalities. The county requested municipalities increase fees to cover the shortfall.

Committee members discussed the Mountain Bay Metro non-dog stray contract, noting costs of approximately $175-200 per animal including mice. One member suggested reviewing contract terms so animals must be brought in by law enforcement rather than residents to provide discretionary oversight.

Staff member Renee reported the farmers market has been going well, with full vendor capacity including coffee vendors, new food trucks, and craft vendors. She noted some Tuesday markets have been affected by cool and windy weather. Trustees discussed parking concerns on Alderson and Newport streets, with staff clarifying that east side of Alderson remains no parking while west side now allows parking.
Trustee Evan announced he is stepping down from the committee as he and his wife are moving away from Weston but remaining in the metro area. Committee members thanked him for his service.

actionItems: CDA to pass resolution to call TIF bonds, anticipated summer 2025
Contact Ellers to notify US Bank of bond call for October 1st deadline
Consider increasing chief election inspector pay from $13 to $15 per hour at next election term
Recruit additional part-time clerk's office staff member
Recruit citizens to fill open committee positions
Next meeting scheduled for Monday, June 15th at 4:30 PM

title: Community Life and Public Safety
overview: The Village of Weston Community Life and Public Safety Committee met to address several regulatory matters, recommending approval of nuisance ordinance amendments, an e-bike ordinance with restrictions on e-motos, mobile food vendor fire inspections, address corrections, and a raze order for an abandoned property. The committee also discussed overnight parking regulations but deferred action pending staff research on problem areas.

agenda.item: Call to order and Pledge of Allegiance
Approval of minutes from previous meeting
Acknowledgment of department reports (Code Enforcement, Fire/EMS, Police)
Overnight parking regulations October 31 to May 1
Mobile food vendors fire inspection requirements

discussions.body: Code Enforcement reported that 3020 Mountain View Avenue will be removed from the enforcement roll after property sale and new owner submitted repair permits. Police Chief Hunt reported 2,394 calls in March (77 per day), 410 traffic stops, and 281 citations. The department has hired its 40th officer, with recent hires starting in phases through January. Two SROs work on zoning violations during summer for Weston, Rothschild, and Schofield. Motion to acknowledge reports 5, 6, and 7 passed unanimously.

The committee reviewed final amendments including tree limb height requirements (14 and 10 feet) that were switched as discussed in previous meetings, plus language requiring compliance with jurisdictional authority. Olsen made motion to recommend approval to board of trustees, seconded by Campkey. Motion passed unanimously.

Extensive discussion focused on e-motos, which Chief Hunt characterized as essentially electric dirt bikes that pose safety hazards. The committee debated whether to allow e-motos on roadways, sidewalks, or trails. Chief Hunt recommended prohibiting e-motos entirely on public property, comparing them to unlicensed dirt bikes. Discussion also covered electric scooters and whether exemptions for children's play scooters were needed. Olsen moved to recommend approval with amendment prohibiting e-motos on roadways, sidewalks, trails, and any public property in the village. Seconded by Chwanka. Motion passed unanimously.

Trustee Tolson presented research showing Weston's 12-hour parking window (4am-4pm) is significantly different from surrounding municipalities, which typically have 5-hour windows overnight. Public Works Director Michael Wodalski explained the daytime restriction helps plow operations but acknowledged most plowing doesn't reach residential streets until 7-8am. Chief Hunt favored keeping overnight restrictions to clear cars before plowing begins. Committee agreed to defer action until July, directing Wodalski to identify 6-8 problem areas that could be specifically signed rather than village-wide restrictions.

Mount Olive Church requested a crosswalk between their facility and DC Everest parking across the street. Committee discussed costs ($2,000-3,000 for basic crosswalk, $25,000-30,000 for lighted system) and whether the village should pay. Chief Hunt reported no incidents in the area. Motion passed directing staff to contact Mount Olive about covering installation costs; if they agree, the crosswalk would be recommended for board approval. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Chwanka, passed unanimously.

Fire Chief presented new regional fire inspection program involving Wisconsin Rapids, Marshfield, Stevens Point, Plover, Rib Mountain, and Weston. One inspection by any participating department counts for all municipalities. Fire Marshal Marty already conducted an inspection that failed due to improper LP piping and unsecured propane cylinders. Wausau opted out to do their own inspections. Motion by Chwanka to approve Ordinance 26-8, seconded by Campkey. Passed unanimously.

Staff presented three address corrections: 5915 Delicoski Street (duplex needing separate addresses), Ross Avenue properties out of sequence with existing apartment building, and 2211 Radkey Avenue for new duplex on private drive. Chief Hunt mentioned additional issue with Weston Villa Apartments having nine addresses for one structure. Motion to recommend board approval passed unanimously.

Property has been abandoned since 2010-2011 and posted since 2021. Previous fire department training burn was canceled due to unsafe structural conditions. Current owners housed chickens in the structure during winter. Owners are current on taxes and preferred the raze order process. Demolition costs will be added to tax roll. Motion by Olsen, seconded by Campkey, passed unanimously.

publicComment: Jim Senalt spoke during public comment criticizing the new farmers market location, predicting the park's grassy areas will be destroyed by summer's end. He questioned parking vehicles on grass potentially violating village code, noted ADA handicap spaces are on grass rather than required firm surfaces, questioned the origin of new food truck fire inspections, raised concerns about mobile food vendor regulations on public property, and criticized long-standing code enforcement cases showing no visible progress.
actionItems: Recommend Chapter 50 nuisance amendments to board of trustees for approval
Recommend e-bike ordinance to board with amendment prohibiting e-motos on all public property
Public Works Director to identify 6-8 specific problem areas for potential parking signage before July meeting
Staff to contact Mount Olive Church about covering crosswalk installation costs
Ordinance 26-8 for mobile food vendor fire inspections recommended for board approval
Address corrections for three properties recommended for board approval
Begin raze order process for 4104 Shore Avenue
Investigate property complaint at Sternberg and South Timber regarding furniture and dumpster

title: Committee of the Whole Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education's Committee of the Whole meeting on May 18, 2026 was scheduled to address multiple action items including the preliminary 2026-27 budget, employee handbook updates, meal price changes, and policy revisions. The board was also expected to consider approval of the Riverview HVAC and roofing project and updates to the activities and athletics code.

agenda.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling (Action Requested)
Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code (Action Requested)

discussions.item: Excellence in Action: Thomas Jefferson Elementary
discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Thomas Jefferson Elementary as part of the Excellence in Action program. No additional details or presenter information were provided in the agenda.

Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present the Employee Handbook updates for the 2026-27 school year in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to review and consider approval of the updated handbook.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the preliminary budget for 2026-27 in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider the initial budget recommendation for the upcoming school year.
Karen Fochs was scheduled to present proposed changes to the 2026-2027 paid meal prices in an estimated 5 minutes. Supporting documentation included a meal price action sheet and PLE tool.

Dr. Jon Tomski and BJ Brandt were scheduled to present suggested changes to the Athletic and Activities Code in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the 2026-2027 Code of Conduct updates.

discussions.item: Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling
discussions.body: Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present a proposed change to Policy 9270 regarding Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling in an estimated 5 minutes. Action was requested for this policy update.

Dr. Jon Tomski was scheduled to present proposed changes to Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code in an estimated 5 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of these policy revisions.

Ryan Urmanski, Director of Buildings and Grounds, was scheduled to present the Riverview HVAC and roofing project in an estimated 10 minutes. The board was expected to consider approval of the RFP for this facility improvement project.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on Employee Handbook Updates for 2026-27
Board was expected to vote on the Preliminary 2026-27 Budget recommendation
Board was expected to vote on 2026-2027 Paid Meal Price Update
Board was expected to vote on Activities & Athletics Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Policy 9270 - Home-Based, Private, or Tribal Schooling revisions
Board was expected to vote on Policy 2431.01 - High School Activities Code changes
Board was expected to vote on Riverview RFP Approval for HVAC and roofing project

title: Special Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, this Special Meeting of the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address potential litigation in closed session under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). The board was expected to hold preliminary discussions regarding a legal matter before potentially reconvening in open session to take any necessary action.

agenda.item: Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes
Reconvene in Open Session, to take further action if necessary and appropriate

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to enter closed session to hold preliminary discussions regarding potential litigation, as permitted under Wisconsin State Statute 19.85(g). This statute allows governmental bodies to convene in closed session when considering matters that could result in legal action against or on behalf of the district.
Following the closed session discussion, the board was expected to reconvene in open session to take any further action deemed necessary and appropriate based on the closed session deliberations.

publicComment: No public comment period was included on this agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to potentially take action following the closed session discussion regarding potential litigation

title: Regular Meeting
overview: Based on the published agenda, the Wausau School District Board of Education was scheduled to address several significant action items at their May 11, 2026 regular meeting, including approval of the 2026-27 budget reconciliation plan, teacher contract renewals, and wage increases for non-teaching staff. The board was also expected to consider renewal of the WAMCS charter school contract for 2026-2031 and enter closed session regarding personnel matters including non-renewal notices and contract evaluations.

agenda.item: Pledge of Allegiance: Jim Bouche, President
Request for Closed Session Pursuant to State Statutes

discussions.body: The board was scheduled to recognize Maine Elementary as part of the district's Excellence in Action program, which typically highlights achievements and initiatives at individual schools.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was expected to present a summary report of all legal counsel expenses incurred during the third quarter of 2025-2026. The report was described as broken down by law firm and by type of legal advice sought, requiring no action as a written report with an estimated 2-minute presentation time.

Elizabeth Channel, Assistant Superintendent of Operations, was scheduled to present the budget reconciliation plan for budgeting purposes, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 3 minutes with action requested from the board.
Tabatha Gundrum was scheduled to present a list of teacher contracts for approval for the 2026-27 school year. The presentation was estimated at 5 minutes with action requested from the board.

Tabatha Gundrum was expected to present a recommendation for a 2.63% wage adjustment for all employee groups in the District except teachers, in accordance with the budget reconciliation plan. The agenda noted that teacher negotiations would commence soon with a separate action at a later date, with this presentation estimated at 5 minutes.

Elizabeth Channel, WAMCS Head of School, was scheduled to present the 2026-2031 contract renewal for the Wausau Area Montessori Charter School, previously introduced at the April Committee of the Whole meeting. The presentation was estimated at 2 minutes with action requested.

The board was scheduled to enter closed session pursuant to Wisconsin Statutes sections 19.85(1)(c), (e), and (g) to discuss final notice of non-renewal and contract evaluation matters, with plans to reconvene in open session to take further action if necessary.

publicComment: A public and student comment period was included on the agenda.
actionItems: Board was expected to vote on approval of the Consent Agenda including appointments, separations, leaves of absence, retirements, meeting minutes, payment of bills, and donations
Action was requested for the 2026-27 Budget Reconciliation Plan
Board was expected to vote on 2026-27 Teacher Contract Approvals
Action was requested for Various Group Wage/Salary Increase of 2.63% for non-teaching staff
Board was expected to vote on WAMCS Charter School Contract Renewal for 2026-2031
Board was expected to potentially take action following closed session regarding non-renewal notices and contract evaluations

title: Water Works Commission Meeting
overview: The Wausau Water Works Commission approved the 2027-2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility, along with two sole source agreements for cross connection control services and polymer chemicals. The commission also received updates on lead service line replacements and wastewater projects.

agenda.item: Consideration of the minutes of the preceding meeting(s) - April 8, 2026 Regular Wausau Water Works Commission Minutes
Director's Reports - Update on Lead Service Line Replacements
Director's Reports - Wastewater Project Updates: Headworks Screening Project, Cherry Street Lift Station and Lift Station Assessment w/ Clark Dietz
Approving the 2027 through 2031 Capital Improvement Projects (CIP) Budget for the Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility
Approving the Sole Source Renewal Agreement for HydroCorp Non-Residential Cross Connection Control (CCC) Program
Approving the Sole Source Request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA Polymers used to thicken solids in the Belt Filter Presses and Gravity Belt Thickeners
Discussion regarding the Wastewater - Final WPDES Permit Issued with a permit term of five years

discussions.body: The commission approved the minutes from the April 8, 2026 regular meeting. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Update on Lead Service Line Replacements
discussions.body: The director provided an update on lead service line replacements. This was an informational report with no action required.

The director reported on wastewater projects including the Headworks Screening Project, Cherry Street Lift Station, and Lift Station Assessment with Clark Dietz. This was an informational report with no action required.
The commission approved the 2027 through 2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for the Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Renewal Agreement for HydroCorp Cross Connection Control Program
discussions.body: The commission approved the sole source renewal agreement for HydroCorp Non-Residential Cross Connection Control Program. The motion passed.

discussions.item: Sole Source Request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA Polymers
discussions.body: The commission approved the sole source request for MCEP9 and K144L-NA polymers used to thicken solids in the Belt Filter Presses and Gravity Belt Thickeners. The motion passed.
The commission discussed the final WPDES permit issued with a five-year term. This was a discussion item with no formal action taken.

actionItems: Implement 2027-2031 Capital Improvement Projects Budget for Drinking Water and Wastewater Utility
Execute sole source renewal agreement with HydroCorp for Non-Residential Cross Connection Control Program
Proceed with sole source procurement of MCEP9 and K144L-NA polymers for wastewater treatment
Next meeting scheduled for June 2, 2026 at 11:00 AM

title: City Council Meeting
overview: The Wausau Common Council approved mayoral appointments to multiple boards and commissions, passed a consent agenda including bike rack request procedures and special assessments for street construction, but saw contentious debate over proposed amendments to council standing rules with multiple failed motions. The council voted 11-0 on consent items while rule suspension votes were divided, with one failing 5-6.

agenda.item: Week of the Young Child Proclamation
Joint Resolution from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Approving the Bike Rack Request Form
Resolution from the Infrastructure & Facilities Committee Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects
Ordinance from the Common Council to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council

Confirming Appointments of the Mayor of the City of Wausau to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board and Ethics Board

discussions.body: The council approved minutes from both the March 24, 2026 and April 14, 2026 regular meetings. The motion passed unanimously 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Tom Neal.

The consent agenda was approved 11-0, moved by Sarah Watson and seconded by Bruce Trueblood. This included the Joint Resolution Approving the Bike Rack Request Form from the Bicycle & Pedestrian Advisory Committee and Infrastructure & Facilities Committee, as well as the Resolution Levying Special Assessments for the 2025 Street Construction Projects.

The council voted to suspend Rule 11(A) regarding referral of ordinances, passing 8-3 with Matt Hoenecke, Lou Larson, and Bruce Trueblood voting no. However, the motion to suspend Rule 21 regarding amending of the rules failed 5-6, with Carol Lukens, Michael Martens, Andrew Wiskowski, Kristin Slonski, Sarah Watson, and Lou Larson voting against.

discussions.item: Ordinance to Amend Wausau Municipal Code Ch. 2.16, Standing Rules of the Common Council
discussions.body: This item saw extensive procedural debate with multiple votes. A motion to approve as amended failed, as did a motion to refer back to the Rules Review Committee. An amendment by Alder Watson subject to attorney review passed, as did a subsequent amendment by Alder Tierney. An amendment proposed by Alder Neal failed.

The council approved the Mayor's appointments to the Historic Preservation Commission, Sustainability, Energy & Environment Committee, Business Improvement District Board, Community Development Authority Board, and Ethics Board. The motion passed.

publicComment: Public comment was included on the agenda both before the business meeting (preregistered citizens) and after the business meeting.

Special assessments levied for 2025 Street Construction Projects
